note_data.title: Immunity
keyPoints: All living organisms are made of cells and use ATP from respiration for energy-requiring processes
Eukaryotic cells contain a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, with ultrastructure visible under microscopy
Key animal cell organelles include nucleus (contains chromatin and nucleolus), rough ER (protein processing), smooth ER (lipid synthesis), and Golgi apparatus (protein/lipid packaging)
Mitochondria have double membranes with cristae and contain small circular DNA and 70S ribosomes for respiration
Plant cells have unique organelles: vacuole (support and storage), cell wall (cellulose protection), chloroplasts (photosynthesis site), and plasmodesmata (cell communication)
Prokaryotic cells are unicellular, 1-5μm diameter, with no membrane-bound organelles and smaller 70S ribosomes
Prokaryotes have circular DNA freely floating in cytoplasm and peptidoglycan cell walls
Viruses are non-living structures with nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) in a protein capsid, sometimes with phospholipid envelope

definitions.definition: Detailed structure of cells obtained by using a microscope
Cells that contain a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles
Folded inner membrane projections in mitochondria
Protective protein coat that encloses viral nucleic acid

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
State similarities and differences between active and passive immunity
Give a sequence of events in logical order with linking words

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the secondary immune response is faster and more effective than the primary immune response. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Memory cells remain in the blood after the primary infection. When the same antigen enters the body again, memory cells recognize it immediately and differentiate rapidly into plasma cells. This produces antibodies much faster and in greater quantities than the primary response.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Memory cells remain in blood after primary infection
Mark 2: Memory cells recognize antigen immediately on re-exposure
Mark 3: Rapid differentiation into plasma cells produces antibodies faster/in greater quantity

commonMistakes: Confusing 70S ribosomes (found in prokaryotes and organelles) with 80S ribosomes (found in eukaryotic cytoplasm)
Forgetting that mitochondria and chloroplasts contain their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes
Mixing up cell wall compositions: peptidoglycan in prokaryotes vs cellulose in plants
Thinking viruses are living organisms when they are non-living structures
examinerTips: Remember that prokaryotic cells are much smaller (1-5μm) than eukaryotic cells and lack membrane-bound organelles
Focus on the key structural differences: circular vs linear DNA, presence/absence of nucleus, and ribosome sizes
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Antibody structure showing Y-shape with two heavy chains, two light chains, variable regions (antigen-binding sites) at the tips, and constant region at the base. Also primary vs secondary immune response graph showing antibody concentration over time.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the differences between active and passive immunity, including examples of each type
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which cells produce antibodies during the secondary immune response
M/J 2022 P2 Q6(c) (4 marks) — students had to describe how vaccination leads to immunity against a specific disease
O/N 2021 P2 Q3(b) (5 marks) — explain why the secondary immune response is faster and stronger than the primary response
M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — identification of the type of white blood cell that produces antibodies

workedExample.question: Explain why a person who has recovered from measles is unlikely to get the disease again. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that this is about acquired immunity after infection
Step 2: Explain the primary immune response - lymphocytes produce antibodies specific to measles antigens
Step 3: Describe memory cell formation - some lymphocytes become memory cells that remain in the body
Step 4: Explain secondary response - if measles virus enters again, memory cells rapidly produce antibodies
workedExample.answer: When the person first had measles, their lymphocytes produced antibodies specific to the measles virus antigens. Some lymphocytes became memory cells which remained in the body after recovery. If the measles virus enters the body again, these memory cells will rapidly recognize the antigens and quickly produce large amounts of antibodies, destroying the virus before symptoms develop.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for antibodies specific to measles antigens; 1 mark for memory cells formed; 1 mark for memory cells remain in body; 1 mark for rapid antibody production prevents disease

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused active and passive immunity' — always state whether immunity is gained through the person's own immune system (active) or by receiving ready-made antibodies (passive)
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often wrote about antibodies without mentioning antigens' — always explain that antibodies are specific to particular antigens when describing immune responses

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates frequently failed to distinguish between primary and secondary immune responses' — always mention that secondary responses are faster, stronger, and involve memory cells
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a graph showing primary and secondary immune response over time. X-axis shows time (weeks), Y-axis shows antibody concentration in blood. Primary response: slow rise to moderate peak after first antigen exposure, then decline. Secondary response: rapid rise to much higher peak after second antigen exposure. Label both antigen exposures, show memory cells persisting between responses, and indicate the faster, stronger nature of secondary response.
note_data.quickRecap: Cells are the basic units of life using ATP for energy processes. Eukaryotic cells have nuclei and membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotic cells lack these features and are much smaller. Plant cells have additional organelles like chloroplasts and vacuoles. Viruses are non-living structures consisting of nucleic acid in protein coats.
